Fix REAL defects in a generated HLD session. Workspace: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/692baf11-4116-4073-b333-d8a8411e97b8_hld/_response.md (+ maybe capacity.py). The adversarial reviewer found these actionable issues — fix EACH directly in _response.md (and capacity.py if the math is wrong, re-running it with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python): 1. [MAJOR] §8 Deep Dive 'avoid double-running', line 600 (and the contradicting predicate at lines 365/427): The 'Good' tier presents the claim SQL as: `UPDATE jobs SET lease_owner=me, lease_expiry=now+30s WHERE job_id=X AND status='SCHEDULED' AND lease_expiry < now()`. This is a genuine SQL correctness bug: a never-leased SCHEDULED job has lease_expiry = NULL, and in SQL `NULL < now()` evaluates to NULL (not TRUE), so the row is excluded. As written, this predicate would NEVER claim a fresh job — the common case. It also contradicts the other two places (lines 365 and 427) which use `status=SCHEDULED AND lease IS NULL`. The two predicates are presented as the same anti-double-run primitive but are not equivalent, and one is wrong. A senior interviewer would catch this immediately in the core correctness mechanism. FIX: Use a predicate that handles both fresh and expired leases: `WHERE status='SCHEDULED' AND (lease_expiry IS NULL OR lease_expiry < now())`. Make the predicate identical everywhere it appears (lines 365, 427, 600) so the claim/cancel-race reasoning stays consistent. CRITICAL for any mermaid finding: rewrite the offending node/edge labels to remove ALL breaking characters — parentheses, colons, semicolons, slashes, quotes, angle brackets, square/curly braces inside label or edge text. Replace ":" and ";" with words, drop parens, use
for line breaks. Keep flowchart TD for multi-path diagrams. Keep all 11 sections intact and fully expanded; keep md > 20,000 chars; no LaTeX. After fixing, re-read your mermaid blocks and confirm every label is renderer-safe. Return what you changed, final mdChars, and final mermaidCount.

content.content:
1	# Design a Distributed Job Scheduler
2	
3	> The problem: build a service that runs jobs at a scheduled time or on a recurring cron schedule, across a fleet of workers, at scale, exactly-once where possible. The heart of it is durable scheduling, finding due jobs cheaply at scale, coordinating workers so nothing double-runs, and getting from at-least-once delivery to effectively-exactly-once execution.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)
8	
9	> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale first, then sketch the API and core entities, draw the high-level design one flow at a time, and then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — finding due jobs at scale, not double-running a job, and surviving a worker dying mid-job. I'll check in with you as I go. The core tension I want to keep in front of us the whole time is this: a scheduler is easy at small scale and brutal at large scale, because the moment many jobs are due at the same instant — the top of every minute — a naive design either melts or runs jobs twice. Sound good?"
10	
11	The one-line frame: **a scheduler is a durable promise — "run this job at time T" — plus a dispatcher that keeps that promise on time, once, even when machines die.** Everything hard flows from "on time" colliding with "once" at scale.
12	
13	### Functional (the core flows — "above the line")
14	
15	**FR1 — Schedule a one-shot job for a future time.** A user submits "run this job at 2026-06-21T15:00:00Z" and we durably remember it.
16	- 🗣️ Plain words: "I can say 'send this reminder email at 3pm tomorrow' and trust it'll actually fire at 3pm, even if every server I'm talking to right now is replaced before then."
17	- ⚡ Why it's hard: the promise must survive crashes, deploys, and a year of waiting — it can't live in memory or a single box's timer.
18	
19	**FR2 — Schedule a recurring (cron) job.** A user submits a cron expression like `*/5 * * * *` ("every 5 minutes") and we keep firing it forever until cancelled.
20	- 🗣️ Plain words: "I can say 'run this report every weekday at 9am' once, and it just keeps happening — I never have to re-register it."
21	- ⚡ Why it's hard: an infinite schedule can't be pre-stored as infinite rows — we have to *materialize* the next occurrence lazily and never skip or double-create one.
22	
23	**FR3 — Dispatch due jobs to workers at the scheduled instant.** When a job's time arrives, hand it to a worker promptly (low latency from due-time to start) and at scale (tens of thousands due in the same second).
24	- 🗣️ Plain words: "When 3pm hits, the job actually starts at 3pm-ish — not at 3:04 because the system was busy — even if ten thousand other jobs are also due at 3pm."
25	- ⚡ Why it's hard: finding "everything due right now" cheaply across 250M jobs, and surviving the thundering herd at the top of every minute.
26	
27	**FR4 — Execute exactly-once where possible, with retries.** A worker runs the job; if it fails, retry with backoff; if it keeps failing, dead-letter it. Never silently drop, and avoid running the same job twice.
28	- 🗣️ Plain words: "If the charge job runs, it charges the card once — not zero times, not twice — and if it errors we try again a few times before giving up loudly."
29	- ⚡ Why it's hard: distributed delivery is naturally *at-least-once*; turning that into *effectively exactly-once* needs leases + idempotency, not wishes.
30	
31	**FR5 — Track job status and cancel.** A user can read a job's state (scheduled, running, succeeded, failed, dead-lettered) and cancel a future or recurring job.
32	- 🗣️ Plain words: "I can check 'did my 3pm job run, and how did it go?' and I can call off a job before it fires."
33	- ⚡ Why it's hard: cancel races the dispatcher — we must cancel a job that may be a millisecond from being leased to a worker.
34	
35	#### Below the line (out of scope)
36	- Authoring/UI for cron expressions, a workflow DAG engine (job-A-then-job-B dependencies) — this is a *scheduler*, not Airflow.
37	- The job *business logic* itself (we invoke a handler/URL; we don't run arbitrary user code sandboxing here).
38	- Sub-second / hard-real-time scheduling (we target seconds, not microseconds).
39	- Multi-tenant billing, quotas UI, and fine-grained RBAC beyond per-tenant isolation.
40	
41	### Non-Functional (talkable targets)
42	
43	**NFR1 — Durability of the schedule: zero lost jobs (RPO ≈ 0 for accepted jobs).** Once we 201-accept a job, it WILL eventually run.
44	- 🗣️ Plain words: "If we told you 'scheduled', that promise is on disk in multiple places — a data-centre fire won't make your 3pm job vanish."
45	- 💥 What breaks without it: a node crash silently eats reminders, payments, and expirations — the single worst failure for a scheduler, and the one that destroys trust fastest.
46	
47	**NFR2 — Dispatch timeliness: p99 due-to-start latency under ~2 seconds at the top of the minute.** Jobs start within a couple seconds of their due time even under the herd.
48	- 🗣️ Plain words: "Your 9:00:00 job starts by about 9:00:02, even when a million other 9am jobs are firing too."
49	- 💥 What breaks without it: SLA-bound jobs (cache expiry, payment captures, alert escalations) fire late and cause downstream incidents.
50	
51	**NFR3 — Exactly-once-effective execution: duplicate rate target < 1 in 1,000,000 runs.** A job's side effect happens once.
52	- 🗣️ Plain words: "We basically never charge a card twice or send a reminder twice; if we must choose, we err toward 'at least once' and dedup, never 'maybe zero times'."
53	- 💥 What breaks without it: double-charges, double-emails, duplicate provisioning — user-visible and sometimes financial harm.
54	
55	**NFR4 — Availability of the control plane: four nines (99.99%).** Scheduling/cancel APIs stay up ~52 min downtime/year; dispatch keeps running through control-plane blips.
56	- 🗣️ Plain words: "The thing that fires jobs basically never stops; the thing that accepts new jobs is allowed a tiny bit more wobble but still rounds to 'always up'."
57	- 💥 What breaks without it: if dispatch stops, time keeps moving and a backlog of due jobs piles up — every minute down is a minute of late jobs to drain.
58	
59	**NFR5 — Scale & cost: ~250M live jobs, ~40K dispatch QPS at peak, run at a few hundred $K/mo, not millions.** Cost and ops are first-class (Amazon bar).
60	- 🗣️ Plain words: "We hold a quarter-billion pending jobs and can fire forty thousand a second at the worst spike, and the worker fleet — not the database — is the bill we watch."
61	- 💥 What breaks without it: an always-on worst-case-sized fleet burns money 24/7 to handle a 2-second spike that happens once a minute.
62	
63	> 💬 "The whole design is a tug-of-war between *on time* and *exactly once* — and the rope snaps at the top of every minute, when thousands of cron jobs all come due at the same instant."
64	
65	> 🎙️ Script: "So functionally this is five flows: schedule a one-shot, schedule a recurring cron, dispatch due jobs to workers on time, execute them exactly-once-ish with retries and a dead-letter, and track-slash-cancel. The non-functionals are where the real bar is: never lose an accepted job — that's durability with an RPO near zero; start jobs within about two seconds of their due time even under a herd; and make side effects happen effectively once, which I'll get by combining at-least-once delivery with idempotent execution. I'm explicitly leaving out workflow DAGs and sub-second scheduling. The constraint I'll keep coming back to is that thousands of jobs come due on the same second, so the design lives or dies on how cheaply we find due jobs and how cleanly we avoid double-running them."
66	
67	---
68	
69	## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions
70	
71	**Q1 — "When you say jobs run 'on time', what tolerance is acceptable — sub-second, a few seconds, or are minutes fine?"**
72	- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "This single answer decides the whole dispatch architecture. Sub-second means I need pre-loaded in-memory timer wheels; a few seconds means I can poll a database every second, which is hugely simpler and cheaper."
73	- ↔️ Design fork:
74	  - If sub-second → in-memory timing wheels per shard, jobs preloaded ahead of time, much tighter coordination.
75	  - If seconds → a polled, time-bucketed durable index; far simpler, what most real schedulers do.
76	- Assumption I'll proceed with: **seconds-level tolerance, p99 due-to-start under ~2s.** It covers reminders, cron, payments, expirations — the 99% case.
77	
78	**Q2 — "What's the delivery guarantee the customer needs: exactly-once, or is at-least-once with idempotent jobs acceptable?"**
79	- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "True exactly-once across a network is essentially impossible; if the customer can make their job idempotent, I can give them effectively-exactly-once cheaply. If they can't, I have to do much more work and still can't fully promise it."
80	- ↔️ Design fork:
81	  - If at-least-once + idempotent jobs OK → leases + dedup keys; clean and standard.
82	  - If they demand hard exactly-once with non-idempotent side effects → I have to push for an idempotency key contract anyway, because the alternative (distributed transactions into their system) isn't realistic.
83	- Assumption I'll proceed with: **at-least-once delivery + an idempotency/execution key contract = effectively exactly-once.**
84	
85	**Q3 — "Roughly how many live scheduled jobs, and what's the peak burst — do many jobs share the same due time?"**
86	- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "If due times are uniformly spread, average QPS is all that matters and a single node could do it. If they clump at the top of the minute — which cron guarantees — peak is 15x the average and forces sharding."
87	- ↔️ Design fork:
88	  - If uniform/low → single durable store, no sharding, simple.
89	  - If clumped at :00 → time-bucketed sharded due-index + herd smoothing; the core of this design.
90	- Assumption I'll proceed with: **~250M live jobs, heavy clumping at :00 → ~40K peak QPS, 15x average. I'll design for the herd.**
91	
92	**Q4 — "Are job payloads small references, or large blobs we have to store and ship to workers?"**
93	- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "If payloads are big, the job store and the dispatch bandwidth become a storage/egress problem; if they're small references or callback URLs, the store stays tiny and the bottleneck is purely dispatch coordination."
94	- ↔️ Design fork:
95	  - If large → payloads go to blob storage (S3), job row holds a pointer; workers fetch lazily.
96	  - If small → inline the payload (a few hundred bytes) in the job row.
97	- Assumption I'll proceed with: **small payloads (a callback target + a few KB args max), big ones go to S3 by reference.**
98	
99	**Q5 — "When a recurring job is still running and its next occurrence comes due, do we overlap, skip, or queue?"**
100	- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "This is a classic correctness trap for cron. A nightly report that runs long shouldn't spawn a second copy that corrupts shared state."
101	- ↔️ Design fork:
102	  - If no-overlap → only materialize the next occurrence after the current one finishes (or hold a per-schedule mutex).
103	  - If overlap-OK → materialize purely by clock, simplest.
104	- Assumption I'll proceed with: **default no-overlap per recurring schedule (skip-or-queue is configurable), to be safe with stateful jobs.**
105	
106	> 🤝 Checkpoint: "My headline assumptions are: seconds-level timeliness, at-least-once plus idempotency keys for effectively-exactly-once, about 250 million live jobs with a heavy top-of-minute herd, small payloads, and no-overlap recurring by default. If any of those is wrong — especially if you actually need sub-second or hard exactly-once — tell me now, because they change the spine of the design. Otherwise I'll size it."
107	
108	---
109	
110	## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)
111	
112	All arithmetic below was computed by writing and running `capacity.py` with the project Python; only rounded results are shown. (QPS = queries/dispatches per second; TTL = time-to-live, how long a cached value lives before it auto-expires.)
113	
114	| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
115	|---|---|---|---|---|
116	| Live job rows | 50M recurring schedules + 200M one-shot inflight | **~250M** | "a quarter-billion live jobs" | Durable store must index 250M rows cheaply |
117	| Avg executions/day | recurring 50M x 4 fires/day = 200M; one-shot 200M ÷ 7-day horizon ≈ 29M; sum ≈ 229M | **~230M/day** | "about a quarter-billion runs a day" | Sets the firehose the dispatcher feeds |
118	| Avg dispatch QPS | 230M ÷ 86,400 s ≈ 2,646 | **~2.6K/s** | "a few thousand a second on average" | Average is *easy* — not the constraint |
119	| **Peak dispatch QPS (herd)** | avg due/min ≈ 159K; 50% are cron-on-the-minute landing on :00 ≈ 79K; spread over ~2s ⇒ ≈ 39.7K/s | **~40K/s** | "forty thousand a second in a 2-second spike" | **THE decision number — forces sharding + herd smoothing** |
120	| Peak:avg ratio | 40K ÷ 2.6K | **~15x** | "fifteen-times burst at the top of the minute" | Fleet must absorb 15x without being sized 15x always-on |
121	| Naive scan vs bucketed read | naive walks 250M rows/poll; 1-min bucket holds ≈ 159K rows | **~1,575x fewer** | "a thousand-x cheaper to read a bucket than scan everything" | Forces time-bucketed due-index, not a full-table scan |
122	| Due-index shards needed | 40K peak ÷ ~5K/s per partition ≈ 8 | **~8 shards** | "about eight partitions on the due-index" | Shard count for the hot due-index |
123	| Workers at peak | 40K/s x 2s avg runtime = 79K concurrent ÷ 50 per worker ≈ 1,587 | **~1.6K workers** | "around fifteen-hundred workers at the spike" | Autoscaling target; dominant cost |
124	| Dedup store memory | 230M keys/day x ~100 B, 24h TTL | **~23 GB** | "a couple dozen gigs of dedup keys" | Fits one Redis cluster easily |
125	| Durable store footprint | 250M x 500 B x3 replicas x1.6 index/WAL | **~0.6 TB** | "well under a terabyte" | Storage is a *non-issue* — a few hundred GB |
126	| Rough monthly cost | ~556 avg workers x ~$200 + ~$0.9K store + ~$6K cache | **~$120K/mo** | "low six figures a month" | Worker compute dominates, not the DB |
127	
128	**The ONE decision-forcing number:** peak dispatch is **~40K/s in a ~2-second herd at the top of the minute**, while a single durable partition tops out around **~5K writes/s**. That 8x gap is what *forces* the entire design: a **time-bucketed, sharded due-index** (so we read only the current bucket, ~1,500x cheaper than scanning) plus **herd smoothing** (jitter + parallel shard pollers). **Flip threshold:** if real peak stays under ~5K/s — e.g. due times are genuinely uniform, no cron-on-the-minute clumping — a single un-sharded Postgres table with a `next_run_at` index handles the whole thing and we should NOT shard. Storage and average QPS are non-issues; I won't spend design budget there.
129	
130	> 🎙️ Script: "The average is boring — about a few thousand dispatches a second — a single box could do that. The number that actually drives the design is the *peak*: cron jobs love round times, so roughly forty thousand jobs come due in a two-second window at the top of every minute, which is about fifteen times the average. A single durable partition only does about five thousand writes a second, so I'm forced to shard the due-index about eight ways and smooth the herd. The good news is storage is nothing — well under a terabyte — so I'll spend my budget on the dispatch hot-path and the worker fleet, which is also where the money goes, roughly low six figures a month dominated by worker compute."
131	
132	---
133	
134	## 4. Core Entities
135	
136	*(First draft of the nouns; full field definitions land in §7.)*
137	
138	**Job (one-shot execution)** — owns a single future execution: its due time, payload reference, current state, retry count, and the idempotency/execution key.
139	- 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Think of it like a single sticky note on a calendar day — 'do this thing at this time' — that we peel off and act on exactly once."
140	- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "How do you stop two dispatchers from grabbing the same Job?" → answer: a Job is *leased* — a dispatcher atomically claims it with an owner-id + lease expiry, so a second claimer sees it's owned and skips.
141	
142	**Schedule (recurring/cron definition)** — owns a cron expression, timezone, the *next* materialized occurrence pointer, and overlap policy. It is the factory that mints Jobs.
143	- 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Like a standing weekly meeting on a calendar — one rule that keeps producing individual calendar entries instead of you adding each one by hand."
144	- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Why not pre-create all future occurrences?" → answer: a recurring schedule is infinite; we *materialize* only the next 1 (or a small lookahead) and create the following one when it fires — bounded storage, never-skip guarantee from the pointer.
145	
146	**Lease (claim/ownership token)** — owns the fact that worker/dispatcher X currently holds Job Y until time T. It's how we coordinate without a lock held across the whole run.
147	- 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Like checking out a library book — you hold it for two weeks; if you don't return it, the librarian assumes you lost it and lets someone else take it."
148	- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "What if the worker holding the lease dies?" → answer: the lease has an expiry; when it lapses, the job becomes claimable again — recovery is automatic, no human paging.
149	
150	**Execution / Attempt (the run record)** — owns one attempt at running a Job: start/end time, outcome (succeeded/failed/timed-out), attempt number, and the dedup key proving idempotency.
151	- 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Like a stamped receipt for each try — even if we try three times, the receipt's key tells the downstream system 'you already did this one'."
152	- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Where does exactly-once actually live?" → answer: here — the Execution carries a deterministic key (`job_id + scheduled_time`) the target/handler uses to dedup, so retries don't double-apply.
153	
154	**DeadLetter (the give-up record)** — owns jobs that exhausted retries, holding the last error and the full attempt history for inspection/replay.
155	- 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Like the 'returned mail' bin — we couldn't deliver after several tries, so we set it aside loudly instead of dropping it silently."
156	- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Why not just drop a job that fails 5 times?" → answer: silent drops are the worst scheduler failure; dead-lettering keeps durability (NFR1) intact and gives ops a place to replay after a fix.
157	
158	> 🎙️ Script: "The nouns are Job for a single execution, Schedule for the recurring cron rule that mints Jobs, Lease for who currently owns a job so we don't double-run, Execution for each individual attempt and where the idempotency key lives, and DeadLetter for the give-up bin. The surprising one people skip is the Lease — it's the entire answer to 'how do you avoid double-running' and 'what happens when a worker dies', so I treat it as a first-class entity, not a column. I'll flesh out the full fields and which store each lives in when I get to the data model."
159	
160	---
161	
162	## 5. API / Interface
163	
164	Default REST over HTTPS. Security rule throughout: **the caller (tenant) is identified from the JWT, never from a client-supplied field; clients never set state, timestamps the server owns, or another tenant's ids.** (JWT = a signed token proving who the caller is, so the server trusts the identity in it, not the request body.)
165	
166	### FR1 — Schedule a one-shot job
167	- **POST `/v1/jobs`** — POST creates new durable state (a new Job), so POST not PUT.
168	- Key request fields:
169	  - `runAt: string (RFC3339)` — when to fire; server validates it's in the future.
170	  - `target: object` — what to run (a callback URL or handler name + small args). The *what*, not the *how*.
171	  - `idempotencyKey: string` — client-supplied key so a retried *create* doesn't make two jobs.
172	  - Absent on purpose: `jobId` (server mints it), `status`/`leaseOwner` (server-owned — a client can't forge state), `tenantId` (taken from JWT, never the body).
173	
174	```
175	// Request
176	POST /v1/jobs
177	Idempotency-Key: create_abc123
178	{ "runAt": "2026-06-21T15:00:00Z",
179	  "target": { "type": "webhook", "url": "https://app/charge", "args": { "invoice": "inv_42" } } }
180	
181	// Response 201
182	{ "jobId": "job_7f3a", "status": "SCHEDULED", "runAt": "2026-06-21T15:00:00Z" }
183	```
184	- One key design decision: **the create is idempotent via `Idempotency-Key`** — a retried POST returns the *same* `job_7f3a`, never a duplicate job.
185	
186	### FR2 — Schedule a recurring job
187	- **POST `/v1/schedules`** — creates a new recurring Schedule entity.
188	- Key fields: `cron: string` ("*/5 * * * *"), `timezone: string` ("America/New_York" — DST correctness lives server-side), `target: object`, `overlapPolicy: enum` (SKIP/QUEUE/ALLOW).
189	
190	```
191	// Request
192	POST /v1/schedules
193	{ "cron": "0 9 * * 1-5", "timezone": "America/New_York",
194	  "target": { "type": "handler", "name": "daily_report" }, "overlapPolicy": "SKIP" }
195	
196	// Response 201
197	{ "scheduleId": "sch_9b1", "status": "ACTIVE", "nextRunAt": "2026-06-22T13:00:00Z" }
198	```
199	- One key design decision: **server computes `nextRunAt` from cron + timezone** (handling DST), so the client can't desync the clock — the server is the source of truth for time.
200	
201	### FR3 — (internal) Dispatch — no public endpoint
202	Dispatch is internal (pollers → queue → workers); there's no public "run now" on the hot path. A worker pulls work via the broker, not an HTTP call, so the herd never hits a public API.
203	
204	### FR4 — Worker reports an execution result (internal control-plane)
205	- **POST `/v1/internal/jobs/{jobId}/result`** — records an Execution outcome; mutates job state, so POST.
206	- Key fields: `leaseToken: string` (proves the worker still owns the job — a stale token is rejected), `outcome: enum` (SUCCEEDED/FAILED/TIMED_OUT), `attempt: int`.
207	
208	```
209	// Request
210	POST /v1/internal/jobs/job_7f3a/result
211	{ "leaseToken": "lease_55:exp_1750518002", "outcome": "FAILED", "attempt": 1, "error": "503 upstream" }
212	
213	// Response 200
214	{ "jobId": "job_7f3a", "status": "SCHEDULED", "nextRetryAt": "2026-06-21T15:00:08Z", "attempt": 1 }
215	```
216	- One key design decision: **the result write is fenced by `leaseToken`** — a zombie worker whose lease already expired (and whose job another worker re-ran) gets its late result rejected, preventing a stale failure from clobbering a fresh success.
217	
218	### FR5 — Get status / cancel
219	- **GET `/v1/jobs/{jobId}`** — read-only, so GET. Returns current state + attempts. `GET /v1/jobs/job_7f3a` → `{ "jobId": "job_7f3a", "status": "SUCCEEDED", "attempts": 1, "ranAt": "2026-06-21T15:00:01Z" }`.
220	- **DELETE `/v1/jobs/{jobId}`** and **DELETE `/v1/schedules/{scheduleId}`** — DELETE removes future state (cancel).
221	- One key design decision: **cancel is a state transition to CANCELLED, not a row delete**, and the dispatcher checks state at claim time — so a cancel that races a due job either wins (job won't dispatch) or, if already leased, is recorded so a recurring schedule mints no further occurrences.
222	
223	> 💬 "Notice the worker never gets work over a public API and never sets its own job state without a valid lease token — that's how we keep both the herd and forgery off the control plane."
224	
225	> 🎙️ Script: "The public surface is small: POST a one-shot job, POST a recurring schedule, GET status, DELETE to cancel. Creates carry an idempotency key so a retried create never makes a duplicate job, and the tenant always comes from the JWT, never the body, so you can't schedule jobs as someone else. Dispatch itself is internal — workers pull from a broker, they don't hit an HTTP endpoint, which keeps the top-of-minute herd off any public API. And every worker result write is fenced by a lease token so a zombie worker can't overwrite a job another worker already finished."
226

229	## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time)
230	
231	Shared palette across all diagrams: blue = clients, green = our services, amber = queues/streams, grey cylinders = datastores, purple = external.
232	
233	### 6.1 "Schedule a one-shot job"
234	
235	The simplest slice: durably persist a promise. The interesting part is making the write both durable AND immediately indexed by due-time.
236	
237	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
238	|---|---|---|---|---|
239	| API Gateway | The front door that authenticates the JWT and routes the request | Need authn + rate-limit + routing in one managed layer | Not per-service auth (duplicated, drifts); not a raw LB (no authn) | One more hop (~1-2ms) |
240	| Scheduler API svc | Stateless service that validates and writes the Job | Owns the create/cancel contract and idempotency dedup | Not writing direct from gateway (no business rules) | Another tier to run, but it's stateless and cheap |
241	| Job Store | Durable database holding all jobs + a due-time index | Need RPO≈0 durability + a cheap "due before now" query | Not in-memory (loses jobs on crash — violates NFR1) | A DB write on the create path (~5-10ms) |
242	
243	> 🎙️ Narration: "A one-shot create comes in, the gateway checks your JWT and rate-limits you, then the stateless Scheduler API validates the time is in the future and writes a durable Job row. The only real decision here is the store: it has to be durable — RPO near zero — because if we said 'scheduled' we can never lose it, so an in-memory queue is out. I'll use a database that gives me both the durable row and a cheap index on due-time."
244	
245	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
246	> **API Gateway** — a managed front door that authenticates and routes every request. Here it checks the JWT and rate-limits so abuse never reaches our services.
247	> **Idempotency key** — a client-supplied id that lets the server recognize a retried request. Here it stops a retried create from making two identical jobs.
248	> **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** — how much data you'd lose if it crashed right now; RPO≈0 means none. Here it's the durability promise behind "scheduled means scheduled".
249

265	**Numbered narration**
266	1. Client POSTs the job. Why: the gateway is the only authenticated entry. State: none yet. What could go wrong: bad JWT → 401 before any work.
267	2. Gateway forwards an authenticated request with tenant resolved. Why: services trust the gateway's identity, not the body. State: none. Risk: rate-limit trip → 429, protecting downstream.
268	3. API checks the idempotency store for this key. Why: a retried create must return the same job. State: none if new. Risk: key seen → return the prior `jobId`, no new row.
269	4. API writes the durable Job row (status SCHEDULED, indexed by `runAt`). Why: the Job Store is the durable source of truth. State: one new persisted job. Risk: write fails → 5xx, client retries with same key (safe).

273	
274	> 🎙️ Script: "Creating a one-shot is just a durable, idempotent write. The gateway authenticates, the API dedups on the idempotency key so retries don't double-create, and we persist a SCHEDULED row indexed by its run time. I'm deliberately keeping this dumb — all the hard engineering is in *reading* these rows efficiently when they come due, which is the next slices."
275	
276	### 6.2 "Schedule a recurring (cron) job"
277	
278	The twist: a cron schedule is infinite, so we can't store infinite rows. We *materialize* the next occurrence only.
279	
280	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
281	|---|---|---|---|---|
282	| Cron materializer | Logic in the Scheduler API that computes the next run from cron + tz and writes ONE upcoming Job | Bounded storage; never skip; DST-correct server-side | Not pre-expanding all occurrences (infinite rows); not client-computed times (clock drift) | A tiny re-materialize step each time it fires |
283	| Schedule Store | Durable rows for the cron rule + `nextRunAt` pointer | Source of truth for the recurring rule and its cursor | Not deriving next-run on the fly with no row (can't cancel/track) | One extra row per schedule (trivial) |
284	
285	> 🎙️ Narration: "For recurring, I store the cron rule once and only ever materialize the *next* single occurrence as a normal Job. When that occurrence fires, I compute the one after it and write that. That keeps storage bounded and guarantees I never skip, because the pointer always advances by exactly one. I compute times server-side from the timezone so daylight-saving is correct and the client can't drift the clock."
286	
287	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
288	> **Cron expression** — a compact rule like `0 9 * * 1-5` meaning "9am on weekdays". Here it's the recurring rule we evaluate to get the next fire time.
289	> **Materialize** — turn an abstract rule into one concrete row. Here we materialize only the *next* occurrence so an infinite schedule uses finite storage.
290	> **Lookahead** — how far ahead we pre-create occurrences. Here it's 1 (just the next), which is the safest no-skip choice.
291	
292	**Recurring — materialize-next loop**
293	```mermaid
294	flowchart TD
295	    Client[Client app]:::client
296	    Client -->|"1 POST v1 schedules"| API[Scheduler API svc]:::svc
297	    API -->|"2 store cron rule"| SchedDB[(Schedule Store)]:::store
298	    API -->|"3 materialize next occurrence"| JobDB[(Job Store)]:::store
299	    Worker[Worker]:::svc -.->|"4 on fire compute next"| API
300	    API -.->|"5 advance pointer write next"| JobDB
301	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
302	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
303	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
304	```
305	↳ reuses existing: Scheduler API svc, Job Store (from §6.1)
306	
307	**Numbered narration**
308	1. Client POSTs a schedule with cron + timezone. Why: API owns cron parsing. State: none yet. Risk: invalid cron → 400 immediately.
309	2. API stores the durable cron rule. Why: the Schedule Store is the recurring source of truth and what cancel targets. State: one ACTIVE schedule. Risk: store down → 5xx, retry.
310	3. API materializes the *next* occurrence as a normal Job. Why: dispatch only understands Jobs, not rules. State: one SCHEDULED job exists. Risk: crash between 2 and 3 → a reconciler (sweeper) re-materializes from `nextRunAt`, so no schedule is left without its next job.
311	4. When that job fires, the worker (via API) computes the following occurrence. Why: advance exactly one to never skip. State: pointer moves forward. Risk: overlap → SKIP policy means we don't materialize the next until the current finishes.
312	5. API writes the next Job and advances `nextRunAt`. Why: keeps the chain alive. State: next job pending. Risk: double-materialize on retry → idempotent on `(scheduleId, occurrenceTime)`.
313	
314	> 💬 Say-while-drawing: "Infinite schedule, finite storage — I only ever hold the *next* occurrence, and a reconciler guarantees the chain never breaks."
315	
316	> 🎙️ Script: "Recurring jobs can't be pre-expanded because they're infinite, so I store the cron rule once and materialize only the next single occurrence as an ordinary job. When it fires, I compute the one after and advance a pointer by exactly one, which is what guarantees I never skip or double-fire. A background reconciler re-materializes if we ever crash mid-advance, and materialization is keyed on schedule-plus-occurrence-time so a retry can't create two."
317	
318	### 6.3 "Dispatch due jobs to workers at the scheduled instant"
319	
320	The heart of the system: cheaply find everything due *now* across 250M rows, and hand it out without two dispatchers grabbing the same job.
321	
322	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
323	|---|---|---|---|---|
324	| Time-bucketed due-index | The Job Store partitioned/keyed by a coarse time bucket (per minute), each bucket itself sharded | Reading the current bucket touches ~159K rows, ~1,500x cheaper than scanning 250M | Not a single global `next_run<=now` scan (walks whole table every poll) | Bucket boundaries need jitter to fight the herd |
325	| Dispatcher (shard poller) | A set of leader-elected pollers, one owning each shard, that claim due jobs | Parallelizes the ~40K peak across ~8 shards; leases prevent double-claim | Not every node polling all shards (N-way duplicate scans + claim contention) | Need leader election per shard |
326	| Dispatch queue | A durable broker (Kafka/SQS) buffering claimed jobs for workers to pull | Decouples bursty dispatch from worker speed; absorbs the 15x herd | Not direct dispatcher→worker RPC (herd would overwhelm workers, no buffering) | Adds a hop + at-least-once delivery (handled by dedup) |
327	| Coordinator | Leader election + shard assignment (etcd/ZooKeeper-style) | One owner per shard ⇒ no two pollers claim the same bucket | Not "everyone polls, race on lease" (wasteful contention at 40K/s) | A coordination dependency to keep healthy |
328	
329	> 🎙️ Narration: "Dispatch is the hard slice. First, I make 'find due jobs' cheap by bucketing the index by time — to find what's due I read just the current minute bucket, not the whole table, which my numbers say is about fifteen-hundred times cheaper. Second, I shard that bucket about eight ways and put one poller in charge of each shard via leader election, so two dispatchers never fight over the same job. Each poller atomically *leases* the jobs it claims — sets an owner and an expiry — then pushes them onto a durable queue. A worker pulls from the queue, not from an HTTP endpoint, which is how the forty-thousand-a-second herd gets absorbed by a buffer instead of melting a service."
330	
331	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
332	> **Time-bucketed index** — grouping jobs by the minute they're due so a poll reads one small bucket. Here it turns a full-table scan into a tiny range read.
333	> **Lease** — claiming a job with an owner-id and an expiry timestamp. Here it's the atomic "I've got this one" that stops double-dispatch.
334	> **Leader election** — a coordinator picks exactly one node to own a shard. Here it ensures one poller per shard so claims don't collide.
335	> **Broker / queue (Kafka or SQS)** — a durable buffer that holds messages until a consumer is ready. Here it absorbs the herd and decouples dispatch speed from worker speed.
336	> **Coordinator (etcd/ZooKeeper)** — a small strongly-consistent store for locks and leadership. Here it assigns shards and elects pollers.
337

362	**Numbered narration**
363	0. Coordinator assigns each shard to exactly one poller. Why: one owner per shard prevents duplicate claims. State: leadership leases set. Risk: poller dies → coordinator reassigns within its lease TTL.
364	1. The owning poller reads the current time-bucket for its shard. Why: cheap range read, not a full scan. State: none yet. Risk: bucket huge (herd) → read in pages with a limit.
365	2. Poller atomically leases each due job (`UPDATE ... WHERE status=SCHEDULED AND lease IS NULL` setting owner+expiry; or `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`). Why: the lease is the anti-double-run primitive. State: jobs now LEASED. Risk: two pollers somehow overlap → the conditional update means only one wins; the other's update affects 0 rows.
366	3. Poller enqueues claimed jobs to the durable broker. Why: buffer the herd, decouple from worker speed. State: messages in queue. Risk: enqueue fails after lease → lease expires, job re-claimed later (at-least-once, deduped downstream).
367	4. A worker pulls a job from the queue. Why: pull-based flow control — workers take what they can handle. State: in-flight on a worker. Risk: worker slow → message stays; queue depth alarms.
368	5. Worker runs the target. Why: workers own execution. State: target side-effect begins. Risk: target down → retry/backoff (§6.4).
369	
370	> 💬 Say-while-drawing: "Two ideas carry this whole slice — read a *bucket* not the *table*, and *lease* each job so exactly one poller owns it."
371	
372	> 🎙️ Script: "To dispatch on time at scale I do two things. I bucket the index by minute and shard it about eight ways, so finding due work is a tiny range read instead of scanning a quarter-billion rows. And I put one leader-elected poller in charge of each shard; it atomically leases each due job — owner plus expiry — so two pollers can never both claim it, then pushes onto a durable queue. Workers pull from that queue, which is what lets a buffer, not a service, absorb the fifteen-times top-of-minute spike."
373	
374	### 6.4 "Execute exactly-once-effective with retries and dead-letter"
375	
376	A worker runs the job; we make the side effect happen effectively once and handle failure loudly.
377	
378	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
379	|---|---|---|---|---|
380	| Idempotency/dedup store | A fast store keyed by execution-id with a TTL, so a re-delivered job is detected | At-least-once delivery + dedup = effectively exactly-once | Not "trust the queue is exactly-once" (no broker truly is end-to-end) | Workers/targets must honor the key |
381	| Retry+backoff policy | Logic that reschedules a failed job with exponential backoff | Transient failures self-heal without human action | Not immediate infinite retry (hammers a down target — retry storm) | Failed jobs run later, not instantly |
382	| Dead-letter store | A bin for jobs that exhausted retries, with full history | Preserves durability (never drop) + gives ops a replay point | Not silent drop (worst scheduler failure) | Needs an ops runbook to drain |
383	
384	> 🎙️ Narration: "Delivery from a queue is naturally at-least-once — a job can be redelivered if a worker crashes after running but before acking. So I don't pretend to have exactly-once delivery; I make execution *idempotent*. Every execution carries a deterministic key — job-id plus scheduled time — and the worker checks a dedup store before applying the side effect, so a redelivery is a no-op. Failures retry with exponential backoff so a flapping target heals itself, and after a few attempts I dead-letter the job loudly rather than dropping it, because a silent drop is the worst thing a scheduler can do."
385	
386	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
387	> **At-least-once delivery** — a message may be delivered more than once but never zero times. Here it's the guarantee we accept from the queue and then dedup on top of.
388	> **Idempotent execution** — running it twice has the same effect as once. Here it's how we turn at-least-once into effectively-exactly-once.
389	> **Exponential backoff** — waiting longer between each retry (8s, 30s, 2m…). Here it stops a retry storm from hammering a temporarily-down target.
390	> **Dead-letter** — a holding bin for messages that failed too many times. Here it preserves the never-drop promise and gives ops a replay point.
391

409	
410	**Numbered narration**
411	1. Worker pulls the job. Why: pull-based flow control. State: in-flight. Risk: redelivery of a previously-run job → step 2 catches it.
412	2. Worker checks the dedup store for the execution key. Why: this is where exactly-once lives. State: none if new. Risk: key present → skip the side effect, ack, done (idempotent no-op).
413	3. Worker runs the target if new, then writes the dedup key. Why: the side effect happens once. State: side effect applied. Risk: crash *after* side effect, *before* dedup write → on redelivery the target's own idempotency (same key) absorbs it.
414	4. Worker reports outcome fenced by the lease token. Why: stale workers can't clobber fresh state. State: outcome recorded. Risk: lease expired → result rejected, the re-leased run is authoritative.
415	5. API transitions state: success → DONE; failure → reschedule at `now + backoff`; exhausted → DeadLetter. Why: failures self-heal or surface loudly, never vanish. State: DONE / rescheduled / dead-lettered. Risk: backoff target still down → eventually dead-letters, ops alarmed.
416	
417	> 💬 Say-while-drawing: "I don't chase exactly-once *delivery* — I accept at-least-once and make *execution* idempotent with a dedup key; that's the whole exactly-once story."
418	
419	> 🎙️ Script: "Workers pull a job and the first thing they do is check a dedup store keyed on job-id-plus-scheduled-time — if it's already there, this is a redelivery and we skip it, which is how at-least-once delivery becomes effectively-exactly-once execution. On failure we reschedule with exponential backoff so a temporarily-down target heals itself, and after a handful of attempts we dead-letter the job with its full history instead of dropping it. Every result write is fenced by the lease token so a zombie worker can't overwrite a job another worker already finished."

421	### 6.5 "Track status and cancel"
422	
423	Read state and cancel — interesting only because cancel races the dispatcher.
424	
425	> ↳ builds on §6.1 and §6.3 diagrams — adds only the read + the CANCELLED transition; no new components.
426	
427	A GET reads the Job/Schedule row directly (or a read replica). A DELETE flips state to CANCELLED. The race: the dispatcher checks `status=SCHEDULED AND lease IS NULL` at claim time, so a cancel that lands before the claim wins (the conditional claim finds no eligible row); a cancel that lands after the lease is recorded and, for recurring, stops further materialization. Either way there's a clean, defined winner — no "cancelled but ran anyway with no record."
428	
429	> 💬 Say-while-drawing: "Cancel is a state flip, not a delete, and the dispatcher's conditional claim is what makes the cancel-vs-dispatch race have a definite winner."
430	
431	> 🎙️ Script: "Status is a direct read of the job row, optionally off a replica. Cancel is a transition to CANCELLED, not a delete, so it races the dispatcher cleanly — the dispatcher only claims jobs that are still SCHEDULED and unleased, so a cancel either beats the claim or is recorded after it, and for recurring it halts the next materialization. No new infrastructure, just one more state and one more guard."

473	## 7. Data Model & Storage
474	
475	### PART A — Entity table
476	
477	| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Partition / shard key | Consistency |
478	|---|---|---|---|---|
479	| Job | `job_id (PK)`, `tenant_id`, `run_at_bucket (shard key)`, `run_at`, `status`, `lease_owner`, `lease_expiry`, `attempt`, `payload_ref`, `exec_key` | Postgres (sharded) or Cassandra | `run_at_bucket` (minute) then hash | Strong within shard (for lease claim) |
480	| Schedule | `schedule_id (PK)`, `tenant_id`, `cron`, `timezone`, `next_run_at`, `overlap_policy`, `status` | Postgres | `schedule_id` hash | Strong (pointer advance must be atomic) |
481	| Execution | `exec_id (PK)`, `job_id`, `attempt`, `outcome`, `started_at`, `ended_at`, `exec_key` | Postgres / append log | `job_id` | Strong on write, eventual on read |
482	| Dedup key | `exec_key (PK)`, `result`, `ttl` | Redis | `exec_key` hash | Strong (atomic SETNX) |
483	| Lease | embedded in Job (`lease_owner`, `lease_expiry`) | (same as Job) | `run_at_bucket` | Strong (conditional update) |
484	| DeadLetter | `job_id (PK)`, `last_error`, `attempts`, `history` | Postgres / object store | `job_id` | Eventual (inspection only) |
485	| Coordinator state | shard→owner, leadership leases | etcd / ZooKeeper | n/a (consensus) | Strongly consistent (linearizable) |
486	
487	### PART B — Storage decision cards
488	
489	**🗄️ Sharded relational DB (Postgres, sharded by time-bucket) — used for: Job, Schedule, Execution, DeadLetter**
490	1. What it is: a relational database that stores rows with strict transactional rules and supports a conditional `UPDATE ... WHERE` that only one transaction can win.
491	2. Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it: the lease claim needs an **atomic conditional update** — "set me as owner only if status is SCHEDULED and unleased" — so exactly one poller wins a job. Row-level locking and `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` give that directly; this is the same primitive Anshul used in production (`FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`) to hand out work to exactly one worker.
492	3. Considered and rejected:
493	   - **DynamoDB** — great KV durability, and conditional writes exist, but cross-row range reads over a time bucket and the sweeper's secondary scans are awkward and costly; vendor lock-in.
494	   - **MongoDB** — flexible, but the strong, contended single-row claim under 40K/s and the multi-row reconcile are better served by battle-tested SQL locking.
495	   - **Plain Redis as the store** — fast but not the durable RPO≈0 source of truth; we use it for dedup, not the promise.
496	4. Trade-off: we must shard manually by `run_at_bucket`; cross-shard joins are out, so we denormalize and let each shard be independently claimable.
497	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "The job store is sharded Postgres because the lease claim is a conditional atomic update — `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` hands each due job to exactly one poller. I looked at Dynamo, but the time-range reads and the reconcile sweep are cleaner in SQL, and the trade-off — manual sharding by time bucket — is fine because each bucket is independent."
498	
499	**🗄️ Redis (in-memory) — used for: Dedup keys, optional hot status cache**
500	1. What it is: an in-memory key-value store with atomic operations and TTLs (auto-expiry).
501	2. Why we picked it — the access pattern: the dedup check is on the hot execution path — a `SETNX` (set-if-not-exists) per run at up to 40K/s — and it must be sub-millisecond; a TTL auto-expires keys after 24h so memory stays at ~23 GB.
502	3. Considered and rejected:
503	   - **Postgres unique constraint for dedup** — works but adds a synchronous DB round-trip to every execution at 40K/s; Redis is an order of magnitude cheaper per op.
504	   - **Bloom filter** — tiny memory, but false positives would *skip a real run* (drop a job) — unacceptable for a scheduler.
505	4. Trade-off: Redis is not the durable source of truth, so we treat a dedup-store loss as "fall back to at-least-once" (a rare extra run), not a correctness break, because targets also honor the key.
506	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Dedup lives in Redis with a 24-hour TTL — a set-if-not-exists per run, sub-millisecond at forty-thousand a second, about twenty gigs of memory. I avoided a Bloom filter because a false positive would *skip* a real job, and skipping is the one thing a scheduler must never do."
507	
508	**🗄️ etcd / ZooKeeper (consensus store) — used for: leader election, shard ownership**
509	1. What it is: a small strongly-consistent store that uses a consensus protocol (Raft/ZAB) so all nodes agree on one truth even during partitions.
510	2. Why we picked it — the access pattern: assigning each shard to exactly one poller requires **linearizable** leadership leases — two nodes can never both believe they own a shard.
511	3. Considered and rejected:
512	   - **A Postgres advisory lock** — works at small scale but isn't built for fast failover/membership across a fleet; etcd's lease + watch model is purpose-built.
513	   - **No coordinator, everyone polls + races on the DB lease** — correct (the DB claim still dedups) but wastes ~8x the read load at 40K/s on duplicate scans.
514	4. Trade-off: one more critical dependency to run and monitor; if it's down, dispatch keeps the *last* assignment (fail-static) rather than thrashing.
515	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Shard ownership lives in etcd because leader election needs linearizable agreement — two pollers can't both own a shard. Could I skip it and let everyone race on the DB lease? Yes, and it'd still be correct, but it'd waste eight times the read load, so I pay for a coordinator."
516	
517	**🗄️ Durable broker (Kafka or SQS) — used for: the dispatch queue**
518	1. What it is: a durable, partitioned log/queue that holds messages until consumers pull them, replicated so nothing is lost.
519	2. Why we picked it — the access pattern: it buffers the 15x top-of-minute herd so workers pull at their own pace and a worker crash just leaves the message for redelivery (at-least-once).
520	3. Considered and rejected:
521	   - **Direct dispatcher→worker RPC** — no buffer; the herd would overwhelm workers and any worker restart drops in-flight work.
522	   - **A second DB table as a queue** — works small, but at 40K/s the polling/locking is exactly what a purpose-built broker already solves.
523	4. Trade-off: at-least-once delivery (handled by the dedup store) and one more hop of latency (~5-20ms), which fits the 2s budget.
524	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Claimed jobs go onto a durable broker so the herd hits a buffer, not a service, and a crashed worker just means redelivery — which my dedup store turns back into exactly-once."
525	
526	### PART C — Per-operation consistency
527	
528	| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right |
529	|---|---|---|---|
530	| Claim/lease a due job | Job Store | Strong (conditional atomic update) | Two pollers must never both win — the anti-double-run invariant |
531	| Advance recurring `next_run_at` | Schedule Store | Strong (atomic) | Must advance exactly one to never skip/double-materialize |
532	| Dedup check on execution | Redis | Strong (atomic SETNX) | Exactly-once-effective depends on this being race-free |
533	| Read job status (GET) | Job Store replica | Eventual (read replica OK) | Status display tolerates a few hundred ms staleness |
534	| Read-your-own-writes after create | Job Store primary | Strong (read primary after write) | A user who just scheduled must see SCHEDULED immediately |
535	| Dead-letter / history read | DeadLetter store | Eventual | Inspection/replay only, not on a hot path |
536	
537	> 🎙️ Script: "The dominant store is sharded Postgres, because the lease claim is a conditional atomic update — `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` — that hands each due job to exactly one poller, and it's the durable RPO-zero source of truth. Redis holds dedup keys with a 24-hour TTL for sub-millisecond exactly-once checks; etcd holds shard ownership because leader election needs linearizable agreement; and a durable broker buffers the herd. I considered DynamoDB for the job store but rejected it because the time-range reads and the reconcile sweep are far cleaner in SQL. Reads like status are fine off a replica, but a just-scheduled job is read from primary so the user sees their own write."

541	## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great
542	
543	> 🆘 If you get stuck: go back to the two invariants — *on time* (find due jobs cheaply, smooth the herd) and *exactly once* (lease + idempotency) — and reason forward from whichever one the question pressures.
544	
545	### How do we find due jobs cheaply at 250M rows?
546	
547	**#### Bad: full-table scan every tick**
548	- 🗣️ Plain words: "Every second, ask the database for everything due now by looking at the whole table."
549	- Approach: `SELECT * FROM jobs WHERE next_run <= now() AND status='SCHEDULED'` on a poll loop.
550	- Why people try this: it's the obvious first query and works fine at 10K jobs.
551	- ⚠️ What breaks: at 250M rows, even with an index the planner walks a huge range; every poller doing this every second is brutal, and concurrent pollers thrash the same hot index. With 8 pollers polling per second, that's ~8 scans/s over a quarter-billion-row index.
552	- 🔁 What forces the upgrade: the moment the table grows past a few million rows, scan cost and index contention blow the 2s latency budget.
553	
554	**#### Good: time-bucketed due-index**
555	- ↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: it read the *whole* table to find a tiny due slice; we make the read proportional to what's actually due, not what exists.
556	- 🗣️ Plain words: "Group jobs into per-minute folders; to find what's due, open just this minute's folder."
557	- Approach: add `run_at_bucket` (truncate `run_at` to the minute) and key/partition by it. A poll reads only the current (and just-past) bucket — ~159K rows instead of 250M.
558	- ⚠️ What breaks: a single bucket is still one hot partition; at the herd it must absorb ~40K claims in 2s, above the ~5K/s a single partition sustains.
559	- 🔁 What forces the upgrade: the top-of-minute herd concentrates all load on one bucket → must shard the bucket.
560	
561	```mermaid
562	flowchart TD
563	    Poller[Dispatcher poller]:::svc -->|"read only current minute"| Bucket[(Minute bucket 09 00)]:::store
564	    Bucket -.->|"ignored not due"| Other[(Other buckets 250M rows)]:::store
565	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
566	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
567	```
568	
569	**#### Great: sharded time-buckets + parallel pollers + jitter**
570	- ↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: one bucket per minute is a single hot partition that the herd overwhelms; we split each bucket into shards owned by parallel pollers.
571	- 🗣️ Plain words: "Split each minute's folder into eight drawers, give each drawer to its own clerk, and stagger when they open so they don't collide at exactly :00."
572	- Approach: shard key = `hash(job_id) % 8` within the minute bucket. The coordinator assigns one poller per shard (leader election). Each poller pages its shard with `LIMIT` and `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`. Add small per-shard jitter so all 8 don't fire at the identical instant.
573	- 🔢 Decision-forcing math: 40K peak ÷ ~5K/s per partition ≈ **8 shards**; reading a bucket touches ~159K rows vs 250M scanned = **~1,575x cheaper**.
574	- ✅ Failure matrix:
575	
576	| Scenario | What happens |
577	|---|---|
578	| Poller crashes mid-page | Coordinator reassigns shard within lease TTL; leases on claimed jobs expire and re-claim |
579	| Bucket bigger than expected | Page with LIMIT; spillover drains in the next poll (seconds late, within budget) |
580	| Clock skew across pollers | Bucket boundaries + a small grace window absorb skew; NTP keeps it bounded |
581	| Two pollers briefly overlap a shard | Conditional claim means only one wins; the other's update hits 0 rows |
582	
583	```mermaid
584	flowchart TD
585	    Coord[Coordinator]:::svc -->|"assign shard owners"| P0
586	    Coord --> P1
587	    P0[Poller shard 0]:::svc -->|"claim with skip locked"| B0[(Bucket shard 0)]:::store
588	    P1[Poller shard 1]:::svc -->|"claim with skip locked"| B1[(Bucket shard 1)]:::store
589	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
590	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
591	```
592	
593	> 🎙️ Script: "I make finding due jobs proportional to what's *due*, not what *exists*. I bucket the index by minute so a poll reads one small folder instead of a quarter-billion rows — about fifteen-hundred times cheaper. Then because cron clumps everything onto :00, that one bucket becomes a hot partition, so I shard it about eight ways — that's forty-thousand peak over five-thousand per partition — and a coordinator gives each shard to exactly one poller using `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` so claims never collide."
594	> 🧠 If they ask "why not an in-memory timer wheel?": "A timer wheel is great for sub-second precision but it's in-memory — a crash loses every pending timer, breaking durability. I keep the source of truth durable and only load the near-term bucket into memory if I ever need sub-second."
595	
596	### How do we avoid double-running a job (the global coordinator)?
597	
598	**#### Good: leased claim with conditional update**
599	- 🗣️ Plain words: "Whoever grabs a job stamps their name and an expiry on it; a second grabber sees it's taken and moves on."
600	- Approach: claiming is `UPDATE jobs SET lease_owner=me, lease_expiry=now+30s WHERE job_id=X AND status='SCHEDULED' AND lease_expiry < now()`. The DB guarantees one winner; the loser's update affects 0 rows.
601	- Why reasonable: it's a single atomic operation, no separate lock service on the hot path.
602	- ⚠️ What breaks: the *claim* is single-run, but the *side effect* can still double if a worker runs the job, crashes before reporting, the lease expires, and another worker re-runs it. The DB lease alone doesn't dedup the side effect.
603	- 🔁 What forces the upgrade: any non-idempotent side effect (a charge) plus a worker crash window = a possible double-apply.
604	
605	```mermaid
606	flowchart TD
607	    PA[Poller A]:::svc -->|"conditional claim wins"| Job[(Job row lease set)]:::store
608	    PB[Poller B]:::svc -->|"conditional claim 0 rows loses"| Job
609	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
610	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
611	```
612	
613	**#### Great: lease + idempotency key + fencing**
614	- ↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: the lease prevents two *claims* but not two *side effects* across a crash; we add an idempotency key the target honors, plus a fencing token so stale results can't win.
615	- 🗣️ Plain words: "Give every run a receipt number; the target only acts on a receipt number once, and any late report from a worker whose turn already passed is thrown away."
616	- Approach: each execution carries `exec_key = job_id + scheduled_time`. The worker (or the target) checks the dedup store / its own idempotency before applying. Worker results carry the lease token; the API rejects a result whose lease has expired (a *fencing token* — a monotonically increasing claim id so a slow zombie's write is recognized as stale and dropped).
617	- 🔢 Decision-forcing math: target duplicate rate < 1 in 1,000,000 — with a 24h dedup TTL and a fencing check, the only residual is a target that ignores the key, which we make a contract.
618	- ✅ Failure matrix:
619	
620	| Scenario | What happens |
621	|---|---|
622	| Worker runs, crashes before report | Lease expires → re-run; dedup key makes the re-run a no-op at the target |
623	| Two workers run same job (lease bug) | Both present the same `exec_key`; target applies once, second is deduped |
624	| Zombie worker reports after lease expiry | Fencing token stale → result rejected; fresh run is authoritative |
625	| Dedup store (Redis) down | Falls back to at-least-once; rare extra run, target idempotency still guards money |
626	
627	```mermaid
628	flowchart TD
629	    W1[Worker run 1]:::svc -->|"exec key setnx wins"| Dedup[(Idempotency store)]:::store
630	    W2[Worker re-run]:::svc -->|"exec key exists no-op"| Dedup
631	    W2 -->|"stale lease report rejected"| API[Scheduler API]:::svc
632	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
633	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
634	```
635	
636	> 🎙️ Script: "True exactly-once delivery across a network is a myth, so I do the achievable thing: at-least-once delivery plus idempotent execution. The lease guarantees one poller *claims* a job, but to stop a double *side effect* across a worker crash, every run carries a deterministic key — job-id plus scheduled-time — that the target dedups on, and worker results are fenced by the lease token so a zombie worker's late report is rejected. That combination gets duplicates under one in a million, and the only residual is a target that ignores the key, which I make a contract."
637	> 🧠 If they ask "why not exactly-once delivery in Kafka?": "Kafka's 'exactly-once' is exactly-once *within Kafka's own transaction* — it doesn't extend to my worker's external side effect, so I still need idempotent execution. The honest design puts dedup at the side effect, not the queue."
638	
639	### How do we survive a worker that dies mid-job?
640	
641	**#### Good: lease expiry + automatic re-claim**
642	- 🗣️ Plain words: "If the clerk holding a job goes silent past the deadline, we assume they dropped it and let someone else pick it up."
643	- Approach: every lease has a short expiry (e.g. 30s) the worker *heartbeats* to extend while running. If the worker dies, it stops heartbeating, the lease lapses, and a poller re-claims the job.
644	- Why reasonable: no human in the loop; recovery is automatic and bounded by the lease TTL.
645	- ⚠️ What breaks: pick the TTL too short and a slow-but-alive job gets stolen and double-run; too long and a dead worker's job is stuck for minutes, blowing the latency budget.
646	- 🔁 What forces the upgrade: long-running jobs need a smarter heartbeat, and re-claim must be safe even mid-run.
647	
648	**#### Great: heartbeated lease + idempotent re-run + visibility timeout**
649	- ↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: a fixed TTL can't tell "slow" from "dead"; heartbeating lets a live worker keep its job and a dead one release fast.
650	- 🗣️ Plain words: "While the clerk is actually working, they keep raising their hand every few seconds; the moment they stop, the job is free — and because every run has a receipt, picking it up again can't double-charge."
651	- Approach: worker extends the lease every ~10s (heartbeat); a missed heartbeat past the expiry frees the job. The re-run is safe because of the idempotency key (previous deep dive). The queue's *visibility timeout* (the broker hides a pulled message until ack or timeout) mirrors the lease so the broker also redelivers on death.
652	- 🔢 Decision-forcing math: lease TTL 30s, heartbeat 10s → a dead worker's job is reclaimable within ~30s worst case; for a job whose runtime > TTL, heartbeats keep it owned indefinitely while alive.
653	- ✅ Failure matrix:
654	
655	| Scenario | What happens |
656	|---|---|
657	| Worker dies mid-run | Heartbeat stops → lease expires in ≤30s → re-claimed → idempotent re-run |
658	| Worker hangs (alive, stuck) | Heartbeat continues → not stolen; a max-attempt/timeout eventually dead-letters |
659	| Network partition isolates worker | Can't heartbeat → job re-claimed; the partitioned worker's later result is fenced out |
660	| Worker finishes but ack lost | Visibility timeout redelivers → dedup key makes it a no-op |
661	
662	```mermaid
663	flowchart TD
664	    W[Worker]:::svc -->|"heartbeat every 10s extend lease"| Job[(Job row lease expiry)]:::store
665	    W -.->|"dies stops heartbeat"| Job
666	    Poller[Poller]:::svc -->|"lease expired re-claim"| Job
667	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
668	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
669	```
670	
671	> 🎙️ Script: "A worker dying mid-job is the normal case, not the exception, so recovery is automatic. While a worker runs, it heartbeats to extend its lease every ten seconds; if it dies it stops, the lease lapses within thirty, and a poller re-claims the job. The re-run is safe because of the idempotency key, and the broker's visibility timeout mirrors the lease so even a lost ack just redelivers into a no-op. Heartbeating is what lets me tell a slow-but-alive job from a dead one, which a fixed timeout can't."
672	> 🧠 If they ask "what if the heartbeat itself is flaky and a live worker loses its lease?": "Then the job is re-run by someone else — and that's *fine*, because idempotency makes the duplicate run a no-op. I'd rather occasionally re-run a live job than ever leave a dead one's job stuck."
673	
674	### How do we handle the thundering herd at the top of the minute?
675	
676	**#### Good: jitter + parallel shard draining**
677	- 🗣️ Plain words: "Instead of every clerk lunging at :00, stagger them by a fraction of a second and split the rush across drawers."
678	- Approach: add per-shard jitter (a few hundred ms) so the 8 pollers don't all hit at the exact instant; each drains its shard in pages.
679	- ⚠️ What breaks: jitter spreads the *poll*, but the workers still face a 15x burst of jobs to *run* — if the fleet is sized for average, it can't absorb the spike, and queue depth explodes.
680	- 🔁 What forces the upgrade: the worker fleet, not the dispatcher, becomes the bottleneck under the herd.
681	
682	**#### Great: durable queue buffer + autoscale + spreading at schedule time**
683	- ↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: it smoothed dispatch but not execution; we put a durable buffer in front of workers and scale them, and nudge schedules off the exact :00 where allowed.
684	- 🗣️ Plain words: "Pour the rush into a big funnel (the queue) that the workers sip from at their own pace, add more workers automatically when the funnel fills, and — where the user doesn't care about the exact second — spread jobs across the minute so the rush is smaller to begin with."
685	- Approach: claimed jobs go onto the durable broker (the funnel); workers pull at their sustainable rate; autoscaling watches queue depth and adds workers; and for schedules without a required exact second, add deterministic spread (`hash(job_id) % 60` seconds) so they don't all land on :00.
686	- 🔢 Decision-forcing math: 40K/s x 2s avg runtime = 79K concurrent ÷ 50 per worker ≈ **1,600 workers at peak**, but the queue lets us run ~556 average workers and scale up for the 2s spike instead of paying for 1,600 always-on — the 15x burst hits a buffer, not the fleet.
687	- ✅ Failure matrix:
688	
689	| Scenario | What happens |
690	|---|---|
691	| Herd arrives, fleet at average size | Queue absorbs the burst; depth alarm triggers autoscale; jobs run seconds late, within budget |
692	| Autoscale lags the spike | Queue depth grows then drains over the minute; p99 stays within ~2s if spread is enabled |
693	| One tenant floods :00 | Per-tenant fairness/rate-limit on the queue prevents one tenant starving others |
694	| Broker partition full | Backpressure to dispatcher; jobs stay leased and re-enqueue next cycle (no loss) |
695	
696	```mermaid
697	flowchart TD
698	    Disp[Dispatcher pollers]:::svc -->|"enqueue herd"| Q[[Dispatch queue buffer]]:::queue
699	    Q -->|"workers sip at own pace"| W[Worker fleet]:::svc
700	    Q -.->|"depth high"| AS[Autoscaler]:::svc
701	    AS -.->|"add workers"| W
702	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
703	    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
704	```
705	
706	> 🎙️ Script: "Cron makes everything come due at :00, so the herd is guaranteed. I attack it three ways. First, the durable queue is a funnel — the burst hits a buffer, not a service, and workers sip at their sustainable rate. Second, I autoscale workers on queue depth, so I run a few hundred on average and scale toward sixteen-hundred only for the two-second spike instead of paying for peak around the clock. Third, where a user doesn't need an exact second, I spread jobs deterministically across the minute so the rush is smaller in the first place. The number that proves it: forty-thousand a second times two-second jobs is seventy-nine thousand concurrent, but the queue means I don't size the fleet for that constantly."
707	> 🧠 If they ask "isn't deterministic spread changing the user's schedule?": "Only for schedules that opt in or don't specify a second — a 9am cron with no seconds field genuinely means 'around 9am', so spreading within the minute is honest; an exact-second job keeps its second."
